text: The laparoscopic view shows a large defect in the hiatus, indicating a hiatus hernia. The stomach is visible at the bottom left of the screen. The surgeon begins to dissect the stomach from surrounding tissues to open up the hiatus for better visualization of the esophagus.

The surgeon mobilizes the stomach, gently dividing small blood vessels and the peritoneal reflection. The left crus muscle is visible in the center of the screen, with the esophagus beginning to appear on the left side. The surgeon moves to the right side, dividing the connection between the liver on the left of the screen and the esophagus and stomach on the right.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic procedure, dividing the connections to the right crus. The esophagus is visible in the center of the screen, with the white band of the posterior vagus nerve appearing just below it.

The surgeon gently separates tissue to identify the oesophagus, which is visible at the front along with the anterior vagus nerve. The oesophagus is mobilized from the left, right, and front, and the surgeon proceeds to mobilize the back. The oesophagus and stomach are positioned below the diaphragm to prevent them from moving into the chest. The crura encircle the oesophagus, visible at the top left and bottom right of the screen. A window is created between the posterior vagus nerve and the oesophagus for LINX device placement.

The surgeon holds the vagus nerve with forceps in the left hand, creating a window between it and the oesophagus for LINX device placement. The oesophagus is fully mobilized, and the surgeon prepares to repair the enlarged hiatus by closing the crura around the oesophagus. The visible gap around the oesophagus is addressed with stitches to ensure proper valve function.

The surgeon places non-absorbable sutures to repair the large hiatus hernia, ensuring the esophagus sits snugly within the crura. Four sutures have been placed, but a fifth is added to close the remaining gap, preventing reflux and ensuring the valve functions effectively. The procedure is performed laparoscopically using three operating ports, including a camera port and two smaller ports for instruments.

The surgeon completes the placement of five sutures inside the abdomen and prepares to insert the LINX device around the esophagus. Using a special measuring device, the surgeon ensures the LINX device fits snugly without compressing the esophagus, incrementally tightening it to determine the correct size for optimal valve function.

The LINX device is positioned behind the oesophagus through the window created between the oesophagus and the posterior vagus nerve. The surgeon manipulates the device into place using small stitches, then locks it with a three-dimensional lock. The operation concludes with the removal of surgical ports and the liver retractor.
The surgeon carefully ensures that the LINX device is securely locked around the esophagus by separating the two locking beads and confirming the lock is secure, marking the end of the operation.
